docs.title: Overview
docs.text: PostgreSQL linter for schema migrations and design best practices.

docs.title: Rules
docs.text: Rules

docs.title: Tutorial
docs.text: This tutorial will walk you through the process of integrating pgRubic's linter into your project.

docs.title: cascade-delete (CT002)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for usage of cascade update.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
Database schema should follow the principle of least surprise which says that every component in a system should behave in a way that most users expect it to behave, and therefore not surprise or astonish them.

Cascading deletes should not cause unexpected loss of data. It is certainly dangerous if deleting a single table row can wipe out half your database.

Are you certain you want to take the chance that someone will delete a single reference entry, unaware that doing so may delete a billion related transactions?

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Almost never.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Restrict

docs.title: cascade-update (CT001)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for usage of cascade update.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: In theory primary key should be static so changes that need cascading should not need to happen. If you find yourself needing this sort of cascaded updates then that is perhaps a "code smell" in your database design.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Almost never.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Restrict

docs.title: identity-generated-by-default (CT003)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for identity generated by default.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: GENERATED BY DEFAULT allows you to specify a value for insert or update on an identity column instead of system generated value. This is usually not what you want for an identity column.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: If you want to be able to specify a value for insert or update on an identity column instead of system generated value.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: GENERATED ALWAYS

docs.title: remove-constraint (CT004)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for removal of constraints.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
Constraints are used to ensure data quality, data integrity, consistency, and adherence to certain business rules.

If a constraint is removed without due consideration, the data in the database can become corrupted.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: You can remove constraints if they are no longer needed or need adjustment. Don't just remove them without due consideration.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: No suggestions.

docs.title: astar (GN028)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for usage of asterisk (*) in column references.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
Specifying the columns in a query explicitly greatly improves clarity and readability. This approach helps developers quickly grasp the purpose of the query and fosters better collaboration.

Also, using (SELECT *) complicates code maintenance. When the table structure changes, such as adding, renaming, or removing columns, queries with SELECT * can fail unexpectedly or silently return incorrect results. By explicitly listing the necessary columns, you ensure the code is more resilient to changes in the database schema.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Almost Never.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Name Columns Explicitly.

docs.title: constant-generated-column (GN016)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for usage of constant generated columns.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: Always generating a constant value for a column is not useful and only leads to duplicated data all over the place and wastage of storage.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: If the table will be storing just one row at any point in time.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Generated column with expression.

docs.title: create-enum (GN006)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for creation of enum.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: Enum types are primarily intended for static sets of values, though there is support for adding new values to an existing enum type, and for renaming values however existing values cannot be removed from an enum type, nor can the sort ordering of such values be changed, short of dropping and re-creating the enum type.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: If your set of values are fixed such that you won't be removing values in the future. Just don't use it automatically without thinking about it.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Mapping table.

docs.title: create-rule (GN002)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for creation of rules.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: Rules are incredibly powerful, but they don't do what they look like they do. They look like they're some conditional logic, but they actually rewrite a query to modify it or add additional queries to it.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Never. While the rewriter is an implementation detail of VIEWs, there is no reason to pry up this cover plate directly.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Don't use rules. If you think you want to, use a trigger instead.

docs.title: current-time (GN020)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for use of CURRENT_TIME function.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: It returns a value of type timetz, for which see TYP002.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Never.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Do not use the CURRENT_TIME function. Use whichever of these is appropriate: - CURRENT_TIMESTAMP or now() if you want a timestamp with time zone, - LOCALTIMESTAMP if you want a timestamp without time zone, - CURRENT_DATE if you want a date, - LOCALTIME if you want a time

docs.title: delete-without-where-clause (GN023)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for DELETE without a WHERE clause.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: Executing a DELETE line without a WHERE clause will remove all the rows in the table which is most likely an accidental mistake and not what you want.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Never.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Add necessary WHERE clause.

docs.title: drop-cascade (GN015)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for usage of cascade update.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
Database schema should follow the principle of least surprise which says that every component in a system should behave in a way that most users expect it to behave, and therefore not surprise or astonish them.

Cascading drops should not cause unexpected loss of data. It is certainly dangerous if dropping a single table can wipe out half your database.

Are you certain you want cascade drop thus dropping every dependent objects?

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Almost never.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Remove the cascade or use restrict

docs.title: duplicate-column (GN009)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for duplicate columns.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: Each column in a table must have a unique name within that table to ensure unambiguous reference and avoid confusion during SQL operations. It ensures data integrity and clarity in database design and manipulation.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Never.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Remove duplicate columns.

docs.title: duplicate-index (GN025)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for duplicate indexes (exact match).

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
Having duplicate indexes can negatively affect performance of database operations in several ways, some of which are as follows:

  • Slower Write Operations
  • Maintenance Overhead
  • Increased Storage Costs

In summary, indexes are not cheap and what is worst is having them duplicated.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Never.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Remove the duplicate.

docs.title: id-column (GN017)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for usage of ID columns.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: The name "id" does not provide much information about what the column represents in the context of the table as it is so genric. Using a more descriptive name can improve clarity, readability, and maintainability of the database schema.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Almost never.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Descriptive name.

docs.title: index-elements-more-than-three (GN005)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for indexes with more than three elements.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
From the documentation:

Multicolumn indexes should be used sparingly. In most situations, an index on a single column is sufficient and saves space and time. Indexes with more than three columns are unlikely to be helpful unless the usage of the table is extremely stylized.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text:
If you really need to.

See https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/indexes-bitmap-scans.html for some discussion of the merits of different index configurations.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Keep your index elements at most three.

docs.title: missing-primary-key (GN004)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for existence of primary keys.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
A primary key is a set of attributes which prevents duplicates and can uniquely identify a record.

If you don't have one, you cannot tell records apart and you create a burden for yourself by checking for duplicate records.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text:
Never for OLTP databases.

If you are doing analytics (OLAP) at scale, then primary keys may not be very useful.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Define a primary key.

docs.title: missing-replace-in-function (GN007)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for replace in function creation.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION simplifies the process of modifying existing functions, as you don't need to manually drop and recreate them.

If you drop and then recreate a function, the new function is not the same entity as the old; you will have to drop existing rules, views, triggers, etc. that refer to the old function. Use CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION to change a function definition without breaking objects that refer to the function. It also maintains data integrity and consistency.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: If you don't need to modify an existing function.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION.

docs.title: missing-replace-in-procedure (GN008)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for replace in procedure creation.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
CREATE OR REPLACE PROCEDURE simplifies the process of modifying existing procedures, as you don't need to manually drop and recreate them.

If you drop and then recreate a procedure, the new procedure is not the same entity as the old; you will have to drop existing rules, views, triggers, etc. that refer to the old procedure. Use CREATE OR REPLACE PROCEDURE to change a procedure definition without breaking objects that refer to the procedure. It also maintains data integrity and consistency.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: If you don't need to modify an existing procedure.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: CREATE OR REPLACE PROCEDURE.

docs.title: missing-replace-in-trigger (GN030)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for replace in trigger creation.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER simplifies the process of modifying existing functions, as you don't need to manually drop and recreate them.

If you drop and then recreate a trigger, the new trigger is not the same entity as the old; you will have to drop existing rules, views, triggers, etc. that refer to the old trigger. Use CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER to change a trigger definition without breaking objects that refer to the trigger. It also maintains data integrity and consistency.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: If you don't need to modify an existing trigger.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER.

docs.title: missing-replace-in-view (GN029)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for replace in view creation.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW simplifies the process of modifying existing functions, as you don't need to manually drop and recreate them.

If you drop and then recreate a view, the new view is not the same entity as the old; you will have to drop existing rules, views, triggers, etc. that refer to the old view. Use CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW to change a view definition without breaking objects that refer to the view. It also maintains data integrity and consistency.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: If you don't need to modify an existing view.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW.

docs.title: missing-required-column (GN011)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for missing required column.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: If a column has been specified as required and you have not defined it, you are probably doing something wrong.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Never.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Define the required column.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: required-columns: List of required columns along with their data types.

docs.title: multi-column-partitioning (GN018)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for partitioning with more than one column.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: One of the main reasons to use partitioning is the improved performance achieved by partition pruning. Pruning in a multi-column partitioned table can easily be sub-optimal, leading to scanning of unnecessary partitions.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: If you know what you are doing and have a good reason to do so. Just don't use multi-column partitioning without deep consideration and knowledge of its intrinsics. An example of what could go wrong is why-isnt-postgres-multicolumn-partition-pruning-smarter-than-this

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Sub-partitioning.

docs.title: not-in (GN026)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for usage of NOT IN.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
Don't use NOT IN, or any combination of NOT and IN such as NOT (x IN (select…))

Two reasons:

  1. NOT IN behaves in unexpected ways if there is a null present: 
    select * from foo where col not in (1,null); -- always returns 0 rows

    select * from foo where foo.col not in (select bar.x from bar); -- returns 0 rows if
any value of bar.x is null

This happens because col IN (1,null) returns TRUE if col=1, and NULL otherwise (i.e. it can never return FALSE). Since NOT (TRUE) is FALSE, but NOT (NULL) is still NULL, there is no way that NOT (col IN (1,null)) (which is the same thing as col NOT IN (1,null)) can return TRUE under any circumstances.

  1. Because of point 1 above, NOT IN (SELECT ...) does not optimize very well. In particular, the planner can't transform it into an anti-join, and so it becomes either a hashed Subplan or a plain Subplan. The hashed subplan is fast, but the planner only allows that plan for small result sets; the plain subplan is horrifically slow (in fact O(N²)). This means that the performance can look good in small-scale tests but then slow down by 5 or more orders of magnitude once a size threshold is crossed; you do not want this to happen.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: NOT IN (list,of,values,...) is mostly safe unless you might have a null in the list (via a parameter or otherwise). So it's sometimes natural and even advisable to use it when excluding specific constant values from a query result.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text:
In most cases, the NULL behavior of NOT IN (SELECT …) is not intentionally desired, and the query can be rewritten using NOT EXISTS (SELECT …): 

select * from foo where not exists (select from bar where foo.col = bar.x);

docs.title: null-comparison (GN024)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for comparison with NULL.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
Comparing NULL to NULL with = returns NULL, not true. Comparing a value to NULL returns neither true nor false, but NULL.

Do not write expression = NULL because NULL is not “equal to” NULL. (The null value represents an unknown value, and it is not known whether two unknown values are equal.)

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Never.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text:
To check whether a value is or is not null, use the predicates:

expression IS NULL
expression IS NOT NULL

docs.title: null-constraint (GN021)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for use of NULL constraint.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
From the documentation:

The NOT NULL constraint has an inverse: the NULL constraint. This does not mean that the column must be null, which would surely be useless. Instead, this simply selects the default behavior that the column might be null. The NULL constraint is not present in the SQL standard and should not be used in portable applications. (It was only added to PostgreSQL to be compatible with some other database systems.)

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Almost Never.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Leave out NULL constraints.

docs.title: nullable-required-column (GN013)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for existence of not null constraint on required columns.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: If a column has been specified as required then it should not be nullable. Having a required column as nullable is an anti-pattern and should be avoided.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Never.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Set the required column as Not Null.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: required-columns: List of required columns along with their data types.

docs.title: required-column-removal (GN012)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for removal of required columns.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: If a column has been specified as required and you are removing it from a table, you are probably doing something wrong.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Never.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Leave the required column.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: required-columns: List of required columns along with their data types.

docs.title: select-into (GN014)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for usage of select into to create a new table.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
The SQL standard uses SELECT INTO to represent selecting values into scalar variables of a host program, rather than creating a new table.

The PostgreSQL usage of SELECT INTO to represent table creation is historical. Some other SQL implementations also use SELECT INTO in this way (but most SQL implementations support CREATE TABLE AS instead). Apart from such compatibility considerations, it is best to use CREATE TABLE AS for this purpose in new code.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Never.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: CREATE TABLE AS.

docs.title: sql-ascii-encoding (GN003)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for SQL_ASCII encoding.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: SQL_ASCII means "no conversions" for the purpose of all encoding conversion functions. That is to say, the original bytes are simply treated as being in the new encoding, subject to validity checks, without any regard for what they mean. Unless extreme care is taken, an SQL_ASCII database will usually end up storing a mixture of many different encodings with no way to recover the original characters reliably.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: If your input data is already in a hopeless mixture of unlabelled encodings, such as IRC channel logs or non-MIME-compliant emails, then SQL_ASCII might be useful as a last resort—but consider using bytea first instead, or whether you could autodetect UTF8 and assume non-UTF8 data is in some specific encoding such as WIN1252.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: UTF8.

docs.title: table-column-conflict (GN010)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for table column conflict.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: While each column in a table must have a unique name within that table to ensure unambiguous reference and avoid confusion during SQL operations, the name of a table should also be distinct from the name of its columns to avoid confusion and ensures clarity in database design and manipulation.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Alsmost never.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Resolve the name conflict.

docs.title: table-inheritance (GN001)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for usage of table inheritance.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: Table inheritance was a part of a fad wherein the database was closely coupled to object-oriented code. It turned out that coupling things that closely didn't actually produce the desired results.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Never …almost. Now that table partitioning is done natively, that common use case for table inheritance has been replaced by a native feature that handles tuple routing, etc., without bespoke code. One of the very few exceptions would be temporal_tables extension if you are in a pinch and want to use that for row versioning in place of a lacking SQL 2011 support. Table inheritance will provide a small shortcut instead of using UNION ALL to get both historical as well as current rows. Even then you ought to be wary of caveats while working with parent table.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Don't use table inheritance. Use declarative partitioning.

docs.title: unlogged-table (GN019)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for use of unlogged tables.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: Unlogged tables are not crash-safe: an unlogged table is automatically truncated after a crash or unclean shutdown. The contents of an unlogged table are also not replicated to standby servers. Any indexes created on an unlogged table are automatically unlogged as well. Any sequences created together with the unlogged table (for identity or serial columns) are also created as unlogged.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: The table is transient and its content can be regenerated after a crash or unclean shutdown.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Use a regular table.

docs.title: update-without-where-clause (GN022)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for UPDATE without a WHERE clause.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: Executing an UPDATE line without a WHERE clause will update all the rows in the table which is most likely an accidental mistake and not what you want.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Never.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Add necessary WHERE clause.

docs.title: yoda-condition (GN027)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for yoda conditions.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
Yoda conditions can be harder to read and understand, especially for those who are not familiar with this syntax.

Placing the constant on the left side makes it harder for someone to quickly grasp the meaning of the condition.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Never.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Natural condition.

docs.title: date-column-without-suffix (NM016)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks that date columns are suffixed with the defined suffix, by default _date.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
Adding _date to a date column name makes it clear that the value represents the date when something happened. For example, created_date indicates the date when a record was created, and updated_date indicates the date when a record was last updated.

Date columns could easily be confused with other types of data if not clearly named. For example, a column named created might be unclear — does it represent a boolean flag, a date, or something else? created_date removes this ambiguity by specifying that it's a date.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Almost Never.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Add the defined suffix or the default _date to to the date column name.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: date-column-suffix: Specify the suffix for date columns.

docs.title: implicit-constraint-name (NM008)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks that constraint is explicitly named and not relying on implicit naming.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
Explicitly naming a constraint is a good practice. It improves your code clarity and makes it more readable.

A good naming convention makes your code easier to read and understand.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Never almost.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Name your constraint according to the set naming convention.

docs.title: invalid-check-constraint-name (NM005)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks that the name of the check constraint to be created is valid according to naming convention.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
Naming conventions are crucial in database design as they offer consistency, clarity, and structure to the organization and accessibility of data within a database.

A good naming convention makes your code easier to read and understand.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Never.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Name your check constraint according to the set name convention.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: regex-constraint-check: Regex matching the naming convention for check constraints.

docs.title: invalid-exclusion-constraint-name (NM006)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks that the name of the exclusion constraint to be created is valid.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
Naming conventions are crucial in database design as they offer consistency, clarity, and structure to the organization and accessibility of data within a database.

A good naming convention makes your code easier to read and understand.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Never.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Name your exclusion constraint according to the set name convention.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: regex-constraint-exclusion: Regex matching the naming convention for exclusion constraints.

docs.title: invalid-foreign-key-name (NM004)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks that the name of the foreign key constraint to be created is valid according to naming convention.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
Naming conventions are crucial in database design as they offer consistency, clarity, and structure to the organization and accessibility of data within a database.

A good naming convention makes your code easier to read and understand.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Never.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Name your foreign key constraint according to the set name convention.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: regex-constraint-foreign-key: Regex matching the naming convention for foreign key constraints.

docs.title: invalid-index-name (NM001)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks that the name of the index to be created is valid according to naming convention.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
Naming conventions are crucial in database design as they offer consistency, clarity, and structure to the organization and accessibility of data within a database.

A good naming convention makes your code easier to read and understand.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Never.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Name your index according to the set name convention.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: regex-index: Regex matching the naming convention for indexes.

docs.title: invalid-partition-name (NM009)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks that the name of the partition to be created is valid according to naming convention.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
Naming conventions are crucial in database design as they offer consistency, clarity, and structure to the organization and accessibility of data within a database.

A good naming convention makes your code easier to read and understand.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Never.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Name your partition according to the set name convention.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: regex-partition: Regex matching the naming convention for check partitions.

docs.title: invalid-primary-key-name (NM002)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks that the name of the primary key constraint to be created is valid according to naming convention.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
Naming conventions are crucial in database design as they offer consistency, clarity, and structure to the organization and accessibility of data within a database.

A good naming convention makes your code easier to read and understand.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Never.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Name your primary key constraint according to the set name convention.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: regex-constraint-primary-key: Regex matching the naming convention for primary key constraints.

docs.title: invalid-sequence-name (NM007)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks that the name of the sequence to be created is valid according to naming convention.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
Naming conventions are crucial in database design as they offer consistency, clarity, and structure to the organization and accessibility of data within a database.

A good naming convention makes your code easier to read and understand.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Never.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Name your sequence according to the set name convention.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: regex-sequence: Regex matching the naming convention for sequences.

docs.title: invalid-unique-key-name (NM003)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks that the name of the unique key constraint to be created is valid according to naming convention.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
Naming conventions are crucial in database design as they offer consistency, clarity, and structure to the organization and accessibility of data within a database.

A good naming convention makes your code easier to read and understand.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Never.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Name your unique key constraint according to the set name convention.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: regex-constraint-unique-key: Regex matching the naming convention for unique key constraints.

docs.title: keyword-identifier (NM011)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for keywords used as identifiers.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
According to the standard, reserved key words are the only real key words; they are never allowed as identifiers. Non-reserved key words only have a special meaning in particular contexts and can be used as identifiers in other contexts.

PostgreSQL won't allow reserved keywords as identifiers without double quotes. This means that if you use reserved keywords as identifiers, you have to always double quote them. That is annoying enough by hand and error-prone.

Eventhough, non-reserved keywords can be used as identifiers in certain contexts, it can be confusing and ambiguious. Also, there is nothing stopping non-reserved keywords from becoming reserved keywords in the future. So, it is best to avoid them altogether.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Never.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Choose a name that is not a keyword.

docs.title: non-snake-case-identifier (NM010)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Check if identifier is not in snake case.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
PostgreSQL folds all names - of tables, columns, functions and everything else - to lower case unless they're "double quoted". So create table Foo() will create a table called foo, while create table "Bar"() will create a table called Bar.

These select commands will work: select * from Foo, select * from foo, select * from "Bar".

These will fail with "no such table": select * from "Foo", select * from Bar, select * from bar.

This means that if you use uppercase characters in your table or column names you have to either always double quote them or never double quote them. That's annoying enough by hand, but when you start using other tools to access the database, some of which always quote all names and some don't, it gets very confusing.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text:
Never ... almost. If it is important that "pretty" names are displaying in report output then you might want to use them. But you can also use column aliases to use lower case names in a table and still get pretty names in the output of a query:

select character_name as "Character Name" from foo.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Stick to using a-z, 0-9 and underscore for names and you never have to worry about quoting them.

docs.title: pg-prefix-identifier (NM013)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for identifiers prefix with pg_.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
From the documentation:

Schema names beginning with pg_ are reserved for system purposes and cannot be created by users.

Since system table names begin with pg_, it is best to avoid such names to ensure that you won't suffer a conflict if some future version defines a system table named the same as your table. (With the default search path, an unqualified reference to your table name would then be resolved as the system table instead.) System tables will continue to follow the convention of having names beginning with pg_, so that they will not conflict with unqualified user-table names so long as users avoid the pg_ prefix.

Same thing applies to other objects such as functions, views, sequences etc.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Never.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Remove prefix pg_ from identifier.

docs.title: single-letter-identifier (NM014)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for usage of single letter identifiers.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: Single letter identifier does not provide much information about what the identifier represents. Using a more descriptive name can improve clarity, readability, and maintainability of the database schema.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Never.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Descriptive name.

docs.title: special-character-in-identifier (NM012)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for identifiers with special characters.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
SQL identifiers must begin with a letter (a-z) or an underscore (_). Subsequent characters in a name can be letters, digits (0-9), or underscores.

PostgreSQL won't allow special characters in identifiers without double quotes. This means that if you use special characters in identifiers, you have to always double quote them. That is annoying enough by hand and error-prone.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Never.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Remove special characters from the identifier.

docs.title: timestamp-column-without-suffix (NM015)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks that timestamp columns are suffixed with the defined suffix, by default _at.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
Adding _at to a timestamp column name makes it clear that the value represents the time when something happened. For example, created_at indicates the time when a record was created, and updated_at indicates the time when a record was last updated.

Timestamp columns could easily be confused with other types of data if not clearly named. For example, a column named created might be unclear — does it represent a boolean flag, a date, or something else? created_at removes this ambiguity by specifying that it's a timestamp.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Almost Never.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Add the defined suffix or the default _at to to the timestamp column name.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: timestamp-column-suffix: Specify the suffix for timestamp columns.

docs.title: disallowed-schema (SM002)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for usage of disallowed schemas.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: If a schema has been included in the disallowed_schemas config, it is not allowed.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Do you really want to use a disallowed schema?

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Allowed schemas.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: disallowed-schemas: List of disallowed schemas.

docs.title: schema-unqualified-object (SM001)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for objects that are schema-qualifiable but are not schema qualified.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: Explicitly specifying schema improves code readability and improves clarity.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: If you really want to not specify schema.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Specify schema.

docs.title: extension-whitelist (ST001)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks that an extension to be created is allowed.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: By default, any extension can be loaded into the database. This is quite dangerous as any bug causing a crash would mean a PostgreSQL would restart. So you not only want to empower CREATE EXTENSION to database owners, you also want to be able to review and explicitly allow extensions.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Almost never. If an extension is not allowed, you are probably doing something wrong.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Extensions that are allowed.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: allowed-extensions: List of allowed extensions.

docs.title: procedural-language-whitelist (ST002)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks that a procedural language to be created is allowed.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: By default, any procedural language can be loaded into the database. This is quite dangerous as some unsafe operations might be introuduced by languages. So you not only want to empower CREATE LANGUAGE to database owners, you also want to be able to review and explicitly allow procedural languages.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Almost never. If a procedural language is not allowed, you are probably doing something wrong.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Procedural languages that are allowed.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: allowed-languages: List of allowed languages.

docs.title: char (TP004)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for usage of char.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
Any string you insert into a char(n) field will be padded with spaces to the declared width. That's probably not what you actually want.

The manual says:

Values of type character are physically padded with spaces to the specified width n, and are stored and displayed that way. However, trailing spaces are treated as semantically insignificant and disregarded when comparing two values of type character. In collations where whitespace is significant, this behavior can produce unexpected results; for example SELECT 'a '::CHAR(2) collate "C" < E'a\n'::CHAR(2) returns true, even though C locale would consider a space to be greater than a newline. Trailing spaces are removed when converting a character value to one of the other string types. Note that trailing spaces are semantically significant in character varying and text values, and when using pattern matching, that is LIKE and regular expressions. That should scare you off it.

The space-padding does waste space, but doesn't make operations on it any faster; in fact the reverse, thanks to the need to strip spaces in many contexts.

It's important to note that from a storage point of view char(n) is not a fixed-width type. The actual number of bytes varies since characters may take more than one byte, and the stored values are therefore treated as variable-length anyway (even though the space padding is included in the storage).

Sometimes people respond to "don't use char(n)" with "but my values must always be exactly N characters long" (e.g. country codes, hashes, or identifiers from some other system). It is still a bad idea to use char(n) even in these cases.

Remember, there is no performance benefit whatsoever to using char(n). In fact the reverse is true. One particular problem that comes up is that if you try and compare a char(n) field against a parameter where the driver has explicitly specified a type of text or varchar, you may be unexpectedly unable to use an index for the comparison. This can be hard to debug since it doesn't show up on manual queries.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: When you're porting very, very old software that uses fixed width fields. Or when you read the snippet from the manual above and think "yes, that makes perfect sense and is a good match for my requirements" rather than gibbering and running away.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text:
1. text
  2. text with a constraint that enforces a maximum string length

docs.title: disallowed-data-type (TP014)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for usage of disallowed data types.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: If you are using a disallowed type, you're probably doing something wrong.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Never. If a data type is intended to be used, it should not be in the disallowed_data_types.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Data types that are not in the disallowed_data_types.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: disallowed_data_types: List of disallowed data types.

docs.title: float (TP011)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for usage of float types.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
Floating point types are inexact, variable-precision numeric types. Inexact means that some values cannot be converted exactly to the internal format and are stored as approximations, so that storing and retrieving a value might show slight discrepancies.

Comparing two floating-point values for equality might not always work as expected.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: When approximates are acceptable, no comparison for equality is needed.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text:
1. numeric
  2. decimal

docs.title: hstore (TP013)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for usage of hstore.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: Hstore is essentially a key/value store directly in Postgres. With hstore you are a little more limited in terms of the datatypes you have: you essentially just get strings. You also don't get any nesting; in short it's a flat key/value datatype.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Hstore can work fine for text based key-value lookups, but in general JSONB can still work great here.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: jsonb.

docs.title: integer (TP009)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for usage of integer.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: Integer can store values up to 2.147 Billion which can lead to integer overflow once the max value is reached. The fire drill when you run out of integers is not cheap.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Tables that store limited lookup options.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: bigint.

docs.title: json (TP008)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for usage of json.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
From the manual:

The json and jsonb data types accept almost identical sets of values as input. The major practical difference is one of efficiency. The json data type stores an exact copy of the input text, which processing functions must reparse on each execution; while jsonb data is stored in a decomposed binary format that makes it slightly slower to input due to added conversion overhead, but significantly faster to process, since no reparsing is needed. jsonb also supports indexing, which can be a significant advantage.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: In general, most applications should prefer to store JSON data as jsonb, unless there are quite specialized needs, such as legacy assumptions about ordering of object keys.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: jsonb.

docs.title: mismatch-column-in-data-type-change (TP018)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for mismatch column in data type change.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: For certain column data type changes, a USING clause must be provided if there is no implicit or assignment cast from old to new type. Logically, the expression in the USING should reference the original column otherwise it is most likely a mistake.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Almost never. When you are sure that the expression in the USING is indeed correct.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: The right column in the USING clause.

docs.title: money (TP006)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for usage of money.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
It's a fixed-point type, implemented as a machine int, so arithmetic with it is fast. But it doesn't handle fractions of a cent (or equivalents in other currencies), it's rounding behaviour is probably not what you want.

It doesn't store a currency with the value, rather assuming that all money columns contain the currency specified by the database's lc_monetary locale setting. If you change the lc_monetary setting for any reason, all money columns will contain the wrong value. That means that if you insert '$10.00' while lc_monetary is set to 'en_US.UTF-8' the value you retrieve may be '10,00 Lei' or '¥1,000' if lc_monetary is changed.

Storing a value as a numeric, possibly with the currency being used in an adjacent column, might be better.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: If you're only working in a single currency, aren't dealing with fractional cents and are only doing addition and subtraction then money might be the right thing.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: numeric

docs.title: nullable-boolean-field (TP017)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for usage of numeric with precision.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: 3 possible values is not a boolean. By allowing nulls in a boolean field, you are turning an intended binary representation (true/false) into a ternary representation (true, false, null). Null is neither 'true' nor 'false'. Allowing nulls in a boolean field is an oversight that leads to unnecessarily ambiguous data.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Never.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: boolean with not null constraint.

docs.title: numeric-with-precision (TP016)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for usage of numeric with precision.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: Because it rounds off the fractional part which can lead to rounding errors slipping in when performing calculations and storing partial results before aggregation. An example of what could go wrong is how-to-prevent-postgresql-from-automatically-rounding-numeric-types

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: When you want to, really. If what you want is a numeric field that will throw an error when you insert too large a value into it, and you do not want to use an explicit check constraint and you always want to store the fractional part in a specific decimal places then numeric(p, s) is a perfectly good type. Just don't use it automatically without thinking about it.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: numeric.

docs.title: serial (TP007)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for usage of serial types.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: The serial types have some weird behaviors that make schema, dependency, and permission management unnecessarily cumbersome.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text:
• If you need support to PostgreSQL older than version 10.
  • In certain combinations with table inheritance (but see there)
  • More generally, if you somehow use the same sequence for multiple tables, although in those cases an explicit declaration might be preferable over the serial types.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: For new applications, identity columns should be used.

docs.title: smallint (TP010)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for usage of smallint.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: Smallint can store values up to 32767 which can lead to integer overflow once the max value is reached. The fire drill when you run out of integers is not cheap.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Tables that store limited lookup options.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: bigint.

docs.title: time-with-time-zone (TP002)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for usage of time with time zone.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
Even the manual tells you it is only implemented for SQL compliance:

The type time with time zone is defined by the SQL standard, but the definition exhibits properties which lead to questionable usefulness. In most cases, a combination of date, time, timestamp without time zone, and timestamp with time zone should provide a complete range of date/time functionality required by any application.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Never.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: timestamptz (also known as timestamp with time zone).

docs.title: timestamp-with-timezone-with-precision (TP003)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for usage of timestamp with time zone with precision.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: Because it rounds off the fractional part rather than truncating it as everyone would expect. This can cause unexpected issues; consider that when you store now() into such a column, you might be storing a value half a second in the future.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Never.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: timestamptz (also known as timestamp with time zone) without precision.

docs.title: timestamp-without-timezone (TP001)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for usage of timestamp without time zone.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
timestamptz (also known as timestamp with time zone) zone records a single moment in time. Despite what the name says it doesn't store a timestamp, just a point in time described as the number of microseconds since January 1st, 2000 in UTC. You can insert values in any timezone and it'll store the point in time that value describes. By default it will display times in your current timezone, but you can use at time zone to display it in other time zones. Because it stores a point in time, it will do the right thing with arithmetic involving timestamps entered in different timezones - including between timestamps from the same statement_location on different sides of a daylight savings time change.

timestamp (also known as timestamp without time zone) does not do any of that, it just stores a date and time you give it. You can think of it being a picture of a calendar and a clock rather than a point in time. Without additional information - the timezone - you don't know what time it records. Because of that, arithmetic between timestamps from different locations or between timestamps from summer and winter may give the wrong answer.

So if what you want to store is a point in time, rather than a picture of a clock, use timestamptz (timestamp with time zone).

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: If you're dealing with timestamps in an abstract way, or just saving and retrieving them from an app, where you aren't going to be doing arithmetic with them then timestamp might be suitable.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: timestamptz (also known as timestamp with time zone).

docs.title: varchar (TP005)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for usage of varchar.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
varchar(n) is a variable width text field that will throw an error if you try and insert a string longer than n characters (not bytes) into it.

varchar (without the (n)) or text are similar, but without the length limit. If you insert the same string into the three field types they will take up exactly the same amount of space, and you won't be able to measure any difference in performance.

If what you really need is a text field with an length limit then varchar(n) is great, but if you pick an arbitrary length and choose varchar(20) for a surname field you are risking production errors in the future when Hubert Blaine Wolfe­schlegel­stein­hausen­berger­dorff signs up for your service.

Some databases do not have a type that can hold arbitrary long text, or if they do it's not as convenient or efficient or well-supported as varchar(n). Users from those databases will often use something like varchar(255) when what they really want is text.

If you need to constrain the value in a field you probably need something more specific than a maximum length - maybe a minimum length too, or a limited set of characters - and a check constraint can do all of those things as well as a maximum string length.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text:
When you want to, really. If what you want is a text field that will throw an error if you insert too long a string into it, and you don't want to use an explicit check constraint then varchar(n) is a perfectly good type. Just don't use it automatically without thinking about it.

Also, the varchar type is in the SQL standard, unlike the text type, so it might be the best choice for writing super-portable applications.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text:
1. text
  2. text with a constraint that enforces a maximum string length

docs.title: wrongly-typed-required-column (TP015)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for wrongly typed required columns.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: If a column has been specified as required and you have typed it wrongly, you are probably doing something wrong.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Never.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Right data types for the required column.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: required-columns: List of required columns along with their data types.

docs.title: xml (TP012)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks for usage of xml.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: Downsides to using XML include slower processing and querying due to its complexity, higher storage requirements and challenges in efficient indexing, rigidity in adapting to schema changes, and the overall complexity in data handling due to XML's verbose and hierarchical structure. These factors suggest that XML can lead to increased resource usage and development difficulties, making formats like JSON more suitable.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: Despite its drawbacks, XML might be suitable when there is a need for data interchange with systems that require XML format, or when dealing with legacy systems where data is already in XML format.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: jsonb.

docs.title: adding-auto-increment-column (US004)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks adding of auto increment column.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: Adding an auto increment column to an already populated table will have to backfill the newly added column, causing the table to be locked in which no other operations can be performed on the table for the duration of the backfill. This will cause downtime if the table is concurrently being accessed by other clients.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: If the table is empty. If the table is not empty but is not being concurrently accessed.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text:
1. Create a new column typed bigint, nullable.
  2. Create a sequence.
  3. Set the next value of the sequence to the total number of rows in the table with enough offset.
  4. Set the default value of the new column to the next value of the sequence.
  5. Backfill the new column for all existing rows.
  6. If the new column is to be set as not null, add a check constraint: CHECK (column IS NOT NULL) NOT VALID
  7. Validate the constraint.
  8. Set the column as NOT NULL.
  9. Drop the constraint.

docs.title: adding-auto-increment-identity-column (US005)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks adding of auto increment identity column.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: Adding an auto increment identity column to an already populated table will have to backfill the newly added column, causing the table to be locked in which no other operations can be performed on the table for the duration of the backfill. This will cause downtime if the table is concurrently being accessed by other clients.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: If the table is empty. If the table is not empty but is not being concurrently accessed.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text:
1. Create a new column typed bigint, nullable.
  2. Create a sequence.
  3. Set the next value of the sequence to the total number of rows in the table with enough offset.
  4. Set the default value of the new column to the next value of the sequence.
  5. Backfill the new column for all existing rows.
  6. Add a check constraint: CHECK (column IS NOT NULL) NOT VALID.
  7. Validate the constraint.
  8. Set the column as NOT NULL
  9. Drop the constraint.
  10. Get the last value of the sequence, with enough offset.
  11. In a single transaction:
    • Drop the default on the new column.
    • Drop the sequence created in step 2.
    • Add GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY constraint instead, specifying the start option as the value from step 10.

docs.title: adding-stored-generated-column (US006)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks adding of stored generated column.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: Adding an stored generated column to an already populated table will have to backfill the newly added column, causing the table to be locked in which no other operations can be performed on the table for the duration of the backfill. This will cause downtime if the table is concurrently being accessed by other clients.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: If the table is empty. If the table is not empty but is not being concurrently accessed.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: A trigger might be a safer option.

docs.title: cluster (US025)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks cluster.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: When a table is being clustered, an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock is acquired on it. This prevents any other database operations (both reads and writes) from operating on the table until the CLUSTER is finished. This will cause downtime if the table is concurrently being accessed by other clients.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: If the table is empty. If the table is not empty but is not being concurrently accessed.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Have a look at pg_repack as an alternative.

docs.title: column-data-type-change (US002)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks column data type change.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
Changing a column's data type requires an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock on the table, preventing both reads and writes. Generally, such change forces a rewrite of the whole table and indexes with every other transactions being blocked for the duration of the rewrite. As an exception to rewrite, when changing the type of an existing column, if the USING clause does not change the column contents and the old type is either binary coercible to the new type or an unconstrained domain over the new type, a table rewrite is not needed. However, indexes must always be rebuilt unless the system can verify that the new index would be logically equivalent to the existing one.

This change might also cause errors and break applications that rely on the column potentially disrupting business operations.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: If the above exception to table and index rewrite is applicable and the clients have been made aware of the new type.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text:
1. Create a new column with the new type, nullable.
  2. Start writing data to the new column.
  3. Copy all data from the old column to the new column.
  4. Migrate clients to the new column.

docs.title: column-rename (US003)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks renaming of column.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
Renaming a column can easily break applications that rely on the column.

If any part of the application code, database procedures, views, or reports use the column, renaming it will cause errors and potentially disrupt business operations.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: If the column is no longer being referenced by clients, probably after migrating clients to a new column.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text:
1. Create a new column with the new name, nullable.
  2. Start writing data to the new column.
  3. Copy all data from the old column to the new column.
  4. Migrate clients to the new column.

docs.title: drop-column (US001)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks dropping of column.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
Not only that mistakenly dropping a column can cause data loss, applications that rely on the column will break.

If any part of the application code, database procedures, views, or reports use the column, dropping it will cause errors and potentially disrupt business operations.

Removing a column from a table may appear to be a reversible action, but it is not. Even when you can recover all the data in the column, you cannot restore the column in a way that makes the table look exactly as it did before.

In postgres, DROP COLUMN form does not physically remove the column, but simply makes it invisible to SQL operations. Subsequent INSERT and UPDATE operations in the table will store a NULL value for the column.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: After updating clients that rely on the column to stop referencing the column and you really want to discard the data in the column.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: You can either keep the column as nullable or drop it once it is no longer being referenced by clients.

docs.title: drop-database (US008)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks dropping of database.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: Not only that mistakenly dropping a database can cause data loss, applications that rely on the data will break.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: If you really want to drop the database.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: No suggestions.

docs.title: drop-schema (US009)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks dropping of schema.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: Not only that mistakenly dropping a schema can cause data loss, applications that rely on the data will break.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: If you really want to drop the schema.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: No suggestions.

docs.title: drop-table (US021)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks drop table.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: Not only that mistakenly dropping a table can cause data loss, applications that rely on the data will break.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: If you really want to drop the table.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: No suggestions.

docs.title: drop-tablespace (US007)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks dropping of tablespace.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: Not only that mistakenly dropping a tablespace can cause data loss, applications that rely on the data will break.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: If you really want to drop the tablespace.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: No suggestions.

docs.title: index-movement-to-tablespace (US017)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks index movement to a tablespace.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: Moving an index to a different tablespace acquires an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock on the table, blocking other accesses until the movement is completed. This will cause downtime if the table is concurrently being accessed by other clients.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: If the table is empty. If the table is not empty but is not being concurrently accessed.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Have a look at pg_repack as an alternative.

docs.title: indexes-movement-to-tablespace (US018)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks indexes movement to a different tablespace.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: Moving indexes to a different tablespace acquires an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock on the respective tables, blocking other accesses until the movement is completed. This will cause downtime if the tables are concurrently being accessed by other clients.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: If the tables are empty. If the tables are not empty but is not being concurrently accessed.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Have a look at pg_repack as an alternative.

docs.title: non-concurrent-detach-partition (US027)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks non-concurrent detach partition.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
Detaching a partition in non-concurrent mode acquires an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock on both the partition and the parent table, blocking other accesses to all partitions until the DETACH PARTITION is completed. This will cause downtime if the partitions are concurrently being accessed by other clients.

Concurrent mode, CONCURRENTLY is a new feature from PostgreSQL 14.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: If the partitioned table is empty. If the partitioned table is not empty but is not being concurrently accessed.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: From PostgreSQL 14, detach the partition in concurrent mode: ALTER TABLE .. DETACH PARTITION .. CONCURRENTLY

docs.title: non-concurrent-index-creation (US016)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks non-concurrent index creation.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: Creating an index in non-concurrent mode will locks out writes (but not reads) on the table until it is done. This will cause downtime if the table is concurrently being written by other clients.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: If the table is empty. If the table is not empty but is not being concurrently written.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Create the index in concurrent mode: CREATE .. INDEX CONCURRENTLY ON ...

docs.title: non-concurrent-index-drop (US019)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks non-concurrent index drop.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: Dropping an index in non-concurrent mode acquires an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock on the table, blocking other accesses until the index drop is completed. This will cause downtime if the table is concurrently being accessed by other clients.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: If the table is empty. If the table is not empty but is not being concurrently accessed.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Drop the index in concurrent mode: DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY ...

docs.title: non-concurrent-refresh-materialized-view (US028)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks non-concurrent refresh materialized view.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: Refreshing a materialized view in non-concurrent mode will locks out reads on the materialized view until it is done. This will cause downtime if the materialized view is concurrently being read by other clients.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: If downtime is acceptable.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text:
Refresh the materialized view in concurrent mode: REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY ..

Please note that CONCURRENTLY option is only allowed if there is at least one UNIQUE index on the materialized view which uses only column names and includes all rows; that is, it must not be an expression index or include a WHERE clause.

docs.title: non-concurrent-reindex (US020)
docs.text:
Automatic fix is available

Non concurrent reindex.

docs.title: not-null-constraint-on-existing-column (US010)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks NOT NULL constraint on an existing column.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: Adding a NOT NULL constraint to an existing column of an already populated table will have to scan and validate that there are no nulls in the column, causing the table to be locked in which no other operations can be performed on the table for the duration of the validation. This will cause downtime if the table is concurrently being accessed by other clients.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: If the table is empty. If the table is not empty but is not being concurrently accessed.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text:
1. Create a check constraint: CHECK (column IS NOT NULL) NOT VALID.
  2. Validate the constraint.
  3. Set the column as NOT NULL.
  4. Drop the constraint.

docs.title: not-null-constraint-on-new-column-with-volatile-default (US011)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks NOT NULL constraint on a new column with volatile default.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: Adding a new column with NOT NULL constraint and a volatile default to an already populated table will have to backfill the newly added column with the default, causing the table to be locked, in which no other operations can be performed on the table for the duration of the backfill. This will cause downtime if the table is concurrently being accessed by other clients.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: If the table is empty. If the table is not empty but is not being concurrently accessed.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text:
1. Create the new column, nullable.
  2. Set the default value of the new column to the volatile default.
  3. Backfill the new column for all existing rows.
  4. Create a check constraint: CHECK (column IS NOT NULL) NOT VALID.
  5. Validate the constraint.
  6. Set the column as NOT NULL.
  7. Drop the constraint.

docs.title: primary-key-constraint-creating-new-index (US015)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks primary key constraint creating new index.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: Primary key constraint is supported by a unique index and a NOT NULL constraint in the background. Adding a primary key constraint to an already populated table will create a unique index in non-concurrent mode, scanning and validating that there are no violating records in the table. This causes the table to be locked, in which no other operations can be performed on the table for the duration of the validation. This will cause downtime if the table is concurrently being accessed by other clients.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: If the table is empty. If the table is not empty but is not being concurrently accessed.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text:
1. Create a unique index in concurrent mode: CREATE UNIQUE INDEX CONCURRENTLY .. ON .. (..).
  2. In case the primary key column(s) are nullable, create a non-validating check constraint: CHECK (column(s) IS NOT NULL) NOT VALID.
  3. Validate the constraint.
  4. Set the primary key column(s) as NOT NULL.
  5. Drop the constraint.
  6. Add the primary key constraint using the index created in step 1: ALTER TABLE .. ADD CONSTRAINT .. PRIMARY KEY USING INDEX ..

docs.title: rename-table (US022)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks renaming of table.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
Renaming a table can easily break applications that rely on the table.

If any part of the application code, database procedures, views, or reports use the table, renaming it will cause errors and potentially disrupt business operations.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: If the table is no longer being accessed by clients, probably after migrating clients to a new table.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text:
1. Create a new table with the new name.
  2. Start writing data to the new table.
  3. Copy all data from the old table to the new table.
  4. Migrate clients to the new table.

docs.title: table-movement-to-tablespace (US023)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks table movement to a tablespace.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: Moving a table to a different tablespace acquires an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock on the table, blocking other accesses until the movement is completed. This will cause downtime if the table is concurrently being accessed by other clients.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: If the table is empty. If the table is not empty but is not being concurrently accessed.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Have a look at pg_repack as an alternative.

docs.title: tables-movement-to-tablespace (US024)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks tables movement to a different tablespace.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: Moving tables to a different tablespace acquires an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock on the respective tables, blocking other accesses until the movement is completed. This will cause downtime if the tables are concurrently being accessed by other clients.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: If the tables are empty. If the tables are not empty but is not being concurrently accessed.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: Have a look at pg_repack as an alternative.

docs.title: truncate-table (US029)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks truncating of table.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text:
Truncating a table can easily break applications that rely on the data in the table.

If any part of the application code, database procedures, views, or reports use the data, truncating it will cause errors and potentially disrupt business operations.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: If the data in the table is no longer needed by clients, probably a test data.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text: No suggestions.

docs.title: unique-constraint-creating-new-index (US014)
docs.text: Automatic fix is not available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks unique constraint creating new index.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: Unique constraint is supported by a unique index in the background. Adding a unique constraint to an already populated table will create a unique index in non-concurrent mode, scanning and validating that there are no violating records in the table. This causes the table to be locked, in which no other operations can be performed on the table for the duration of the validation. This will cause downtime if the table is concurrently being accessed by other clients.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: If the table is empty. If the table is not empty but is not being concurrently accessed.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text:
1. Create a unique index in concurrent mode: CREATE UNIQUE INDEX CONCURRENTLY .. ON .. (..).
  2. Add the unique constraint using the index created in step 1: ALTER TABLE .. ADD CONSTRAINT .. UNIQUE USING INDEX ..

docs.title: vacuum-full (US026)
docs.text:
Automatic fix is not available

Vacuum full.

docs.title: validating-check-constraint-on-existing-rows (US013)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks validating check constraint on existing rows.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: Adding a check constraint to an already populated table will have to scan and validate that there are no violating records in the table, causing the table to be locked in which no other operations can be performed on the table for the duration of the validation. This will cause downtime if the table is concurrently being accessed by other clients.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: If the table is empty. If the table is not empty but is not being concurrently accessed.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text:
1. Add the check constraint without validation.
  2. Validate the constraint in a separate transaction.

docs.title: validating-foreign-key-constraint-on-existing-rows (US012)
docs.text: Automatic fix is available

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks validating foreign key constraint on existing rows.

docs.title: Why not?
docs.text: Adding a foreign key constraint to an already populated table will have to scan and validate that there are no violating records in the table, causing the table to be locked in which no other operations can be performed on the table for the duration of the validation. This will cause downtime if the table is concurrently being accessed by other clients.

docs.title: When should you?
docs.text: If the table is empty. If the table is not empty but is not being concurrently accessed.

docs.title: Use instead:
docs.text:
1. Add the foreign key constraint without validation.
  2. Validate the constraint in a separate transaction.
